input.background: The term critical value is often used to refer to the value of a test statistic that determines statistical significance at some fixed α level for a test. For example, ±1.96 are the critical values for a two-tailed z test at α=0.05.
input.question: In performing a t test based on 21 observations, what are the critical values for a one-tailed test when α=0.05? That is, what values of the t_stat will give a one-sided P-value that is less than or equal to 0.05?

output.answer: First note that df=21-1=20. For a one-sided test we get a P-value less than 0.05 when the t_stat is either less than -1.725 or more than 1.725, that is, |t_stat| ≥ 1.725.

alpha_level.description: The significance level for the test, indicating the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true.

sample_size.description: The number of observations in the sample used for the t test.

degrees_of_freedom.description: The degrees of freedom for the t test, calculated as the sample size minus one.

t_stat_critical_value.value: Calculated value based on t-distribution table
t_stat_critical_value.description: The critical value of the t statistic for a one-tailed test at the given alpha level and degrees of freedom.

meta_info.caption: Critical values for a t statistic.
